subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: governance/rights/politics built from independent sources (IMF, World Bank, AfDB, V-Dem, Freedom House, RSF, HRW, Amnesty, BTI, ICG) + international wire (Reuters/AFP/AP/Al Jazeera); Guinean state media EXCLUDED there.

A junta that overstayed its transition: Col. Mamadi Doumbouya seized power in the Sept 2021 coup, banned protest in 2022, ran a Sept 2025 referendum (~90% 'yes') letting military leaders run for office and extending the term to 7 years, then won the Dec 2025 presidential vote (~87%) the UN flagged for intimidation - a coup laundered into elected rule.

Standout flags: a resource-boom-into-autocracy story - the world-class Simandou iron-ore project shipped first ore (Dec 2025) and Guinea is a top-three global bauxite supplier, while opposition leaders are forcibly disappeared (FNDC's Sylla and Bah missing since 2024-25) and the press is sharply repressed (the single biggest RSF Index drop, -25 places, in 2025).
Currency: the Guinean franc (GNF) is a sovereign currency run by the BCRG - monetary independence is judged on that central bank's own credibility, not a peg; not a reserve currency.

economic.notes: A fast-growing mining economy (bauxite plus the launching Simandou iron ore) with improving inflation, set against a sovereign-currency credibility question and an acute resource-curse governance risk.

fiscal_state.notes: Deficit narrowing to ~4.1% of GDP with debt rising to ~48% of GDP in 2025; no IMF program (a 2024 ECF request was effectively stalled over the slow democratic return). (World Bank; AfDB; IMF DataMapper)

monetary_independence.notes: A sovereign currency (GNF) run by the BCRG; policy credibility is limited and historically tied to the political cycle, though inflation management has improved. (BCRG; IMF; World Bank)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The Guinean franc is not an international reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Easing to ~3-7% in 2025 from low-double-digits earlier, on softer food and transport prices. (World Bank; AfDB)

banking_stability.notes: Shallow, low-penetration banking sector with limited supervisory depth. (IMF; World Bank)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Strong export trajectory - a top global bauxite supplier (~80% of FX) now joined by Simandou iron ore (first shipment Dec 2025) - but an acute resource-curse profile with weak governance over rents. (World Bank; Reuters; AfDB)

institutional.notes: Junta rule with no judicial independence, captured transition institutions, and one of the world's fastest-deteriorating press environments.

rule_of_law.notes: Military rule with no judicial independence, enforced disappearances of opposition figures, and entrenched corruption. (Freedom House; HRW; V-Dem; TI)

statistical_integrity.notes: Weak capacity; macro data engaged with IFIs but governance and political data unreliable. (IMF; World Bank)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Limited technocratic depth, heavily politicized under the junta. (World Bank; BTI)

press_freedom.notes: Sharp deterioration - radio/TV outlets censored, journalist Habib Marouane Camara abducted/missing; the largest single RSF Index drop in 2025. (RSF; CPJ)

political_social.notes: A junta entrenching itself through a captured referendum and election, repressing protest, against deep mining-displacement and ethnic tensions.

political_stability.notes: The 2021 coup leader cemented power via a 2025 referendum and an ~87% December 2025 win the UN flagged for intimidation - suppressed, not resolved, instability. (Africa Center; Al Jazeera; Reuters)

civil_liberties.notes: Protest banned since 2022; demonstrations met with arrests, injuries, and deaths; opposition activists disappeared and tortured. (HRW; Amnesty; CIVICUS)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: No clear pathways and weak due process under junta rule; not a significant migration destination. (Freedom House; inference)

social_cohesion.notes: Ethnic-political fault lines (Peul/Malinke/Susu) plus mining-driven land loss and water damage fueling local grievances in Boke. (BTI; HRW)

demographics.notes: Young, growing population - a potential dividend contingent on jobs and governance. (World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: Non-aligned and not at war, courting Chinese and Gulf mining capital while drifting from Western partners post-coup.

alliance_reliability.notes: Post-coup distancing from ECOWAS/Western partners, deepening China/Gulf mining ties (Simandou ore flows to China) - transactional, not a stable alliance. (Reuters; ICG)

conflict_involvement.notes: No active armed conflict and outside the core Sahel jihadist theatre, though coastal-West-Africa spillover is a watch item. (ACLED; ICG)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: No broad sanctions regime; some post-coup targeted measures and FX-management frictions around mining rents. (Reuters; State Dept ICS)

physical_practical.notes: Weak healthcare and infrastructure (mining corridors aside), notable crime/insecurity, and tropical climate exposure.

climate.notes: Heavy-rainfall flooding, coastal exposure, mining-degraded land, weak readiness. (ND-GAIN; WB)

healthcare.notes: Weak, low-spend system with poor outcomes and limited reach outside Conakry (an Ebola-affected legacy). (WHO; World Bank)

infrastructure.notes: Poor power and roads outside new mining corridors; Simandou's railway/port build is a localized upgrade, not nationwide. (World Bank; Reuters)

crime_safety.notes: Elevated urban crime and periodic protest-related violence and security-force abuses. (Freedom House; OSAC)

summary: A resource-rich West African state whose 2021 coup leader, Col. Mamadi Doumbouya, has converted a stalled transition into entrenched rule - a 2025 referendum let military men run and lengthened the term, and he won an ~87% December 2025 vote the UN flagged for intimidation. The economic story is genuinely strong and improving: ~7%+ growth, easing inflation, a top-tier global bauxite position, and the long-delayed world-class Simandou iron-ore project shipping first ore in December 2025 - which gives the economic axis a rare positive skew. But that boom sits atop autocratic governance, with no judicial independence, opposition leaders forcibly disappeared, banned protests met by lethal force, and the world's sharpest 2025 press-freedom decline. The net forward picture is negative: a paradox-of-plenty where mineral upside is captured by an unaccountable junta, leaving rights, rule of law, and resource rents the dominant risks.
